text: DRAFT. PRINCIPLED VS. STRATEGIC NONVIOLENCE The principal difference between these two types of nonviolent movements is rooted in their approach to power and governance. As we explored earlier, strategic nonviolence is a very pragmatic and very effective way of toppiling a dictator or an authoritarian state. However, it is not sufficient for a more profound transformation of the state in the sense of changing the power relations and structures of society in a progressive way. Strategic nonviolent movements typically retain a fairly Machiavellian view of power and the state, the view developed by Max Weber in Politics as a Vocation, which views power as a coercive phenomenon. Consequently, their objective is to take power and to govern society by using the same state apparatus that the dictators had before them and, therefore, by employing the same coercive power. This approach inevitably results in the transformation of any nonviolent movement into a power struggle and in the subsequent corruption of its founding principles. In contrast, a radical pacifist approach to power and governance is essential to creating emancipatory forms of society, politics and economy that transcend the Machiavellian principles that inform state and power relations under authoritarianism and even in democracies.
